articles.headline: To Our Patrons -- founding editorial of Freedom's Journal ('We wish to plead our own cause')
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish and John B. Russwurm
articles.summary: The founding editorial of Freedom's Journal, America's first Black-owned and Black-edited newspaper (New York, March 16, 1827). The editors announce their intention to 'plead our own cause,' arguing that free people of colour have too long been misrepresented by others in the press and pulpit; they pledge the paper to the moral, religious, civil, and literary improvement of 'our brethren,' to combating prejudice through a candid representation of facts, to publicizing useful knowledge about Africa, and to advocating for education and the elective franchise. Signed 'THE EDITORS' at the close of the piece (identified via the accompanying Prospectus as Samuel E. Cornish and John B. Russwurm).
articles.notes: The literal founding document of the first African American newspaper in the United States.

articles.headline: Prospectus of the Freedom's Journal
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish and John B. Russwurm
articles.summary: Formal prospectus for the new paper, published every Friday at No. 5 Varick-street, New York, at $3/year. States the paper's mission to serve as 'a channel of communication' for 'five hundred thousand free people of colour,' to remain non-sectarian in religion and non-partisan in politics while regarding the U.S. Constitution as its polar star, and to urge readers to use their right to the elective franchise. Signed by editors and proprietors Samuel E. Cornish and John B. Russwurm, with supporting testimonial letters from Rev. Samuel H. Cox (pastor, Laight-street Church, New York) and Thomas Eddy, both dated Jan. 17, 1827.
articles.notes: Companion piece to the founding editorial on the same page.

articles.headline: Boston meeting of the People of Colour in aid of the Freedom's Journal
articles.summary: Report of a Feb. 20, 1827 meeting of the People of Colour in Boston, held at the house of Mr. David Walker, to consider giving aid and support to the new Freedom's Journal. James Gould was called to the chair and George B. Holmes appointed secretary; the meeting was addressed by Messrs. Walker, Brown, and Hilton, and Rev. Thomas Paul. Resolutions passed pledging aid, support, and increased subscription patronage; proceedings ordered transmitted for publication in the paper's first number.
articles.notes: Held at the home of David Walker, later author of the 1829 'Appeal to the Coloured Citizens of the World.'

articles.headline: School for Coloured Children of both Sexes, under St. Philip's Church
articles.summary: Recurring advertisement for a New York school for colored children under St. Philip's Church, teaching reading, writing, arithmetic, English grammar, geography (with maps and globes), and history, at $2-4/quarter. References given: Rev. Peter Williams, Rev. James Varick, Rev. S. E. Cornish, Rev. Benjamin Paul, and Rev. William Miller.
articles.notes: Recurs in the March 23 and April 13 issues with the same reference list.

articles.headline: Memoirs of Capt. Paul Cuffee (Part 1)
articles.summary: Opening installment (reprinted from the Liverpool Mercury) of a biographical memoir of Captain Paul Cuffee, the free Black Massachusetts shipowner, merchant, and Sierra Leone colonization advocate. Covers his father's enslavement and purchase by a man named Slocum in Massachusetts, his parents Cuffee and Ruth (of Wampanoag descent), the family's ten children (eldest sons David, Jonathan, and John farming near West Point), Paul's birth in 1759 on Cuttyhunk Island, his early voyages and capture by a British ship during the Revolution, and the 1780 Massachusetts tax-and-representation petition he and his brother John brought before the state legislature, which succeeded in extending citizenship rights to free Black taxpayers.
articles.notes: Continued in the March 23 and March 30 issues (marked 'To be Continued').

articles.headline: Rev. Abraham Thompson memorial notice
articles.summary: Tribute to the late Rev. Abraham Thompson, describing his life as 'honorable to his profession, and creditable to his brethren of colour,' accompanied by an extract on his final illness and death (composure, cheerfulness, and religious faith to the end). The editors promise a fuller biographical sketch from the same author in a future number.

articles.headline: Biographical sketch of Phillis Wheatley
articles.author: S.N. (signed initials)
articles.summary: Short account of the poet Phillis Wheatley: brought from Africa in 1761 and purchased by John Wheatly of Boston at about age seven or eight, taught to read and write English and later Latin, published her volume of poems in 1772 at age nineteen. Quotes two of her poems in full ('On the Death of an Infant' and an extract from her hymn to the morning). Recounts her 1778 marriage to 'Doctor Peter' (a free Black man who studied law) and her death in poverty at twenty-eight after his reported ill-treatment of her. Draws on the Abbe Gregoire's earlier notice of Wheatley as a source.
articles.notes: One of the earliest post-Revolutionary biographical treatments of Wheatley printed in the Black press.

articles.headline: Gerrit Smith's proposed seminary for young men of colour
articles.summary: Report that Gerrit Smith, Esq. of Peterboro, N.Y., intends to establish a seminary during the coming season for the education of pious young men of colour seeking to qualify for the Gospel ministry; the notice solicits information on candidates' names, residence, age, character, and religious standing. Also references the earlier bequest of the late Abiel Smith of Boston toward the African School there.
articles.notes: Gerrit Smith later became one of the most prominent white abolitionist philanthropists of the antebellum period (funder of the 'Smith lands' Black settlement in the Adirondacks and backer of John Brown).

articles.headline: Land for Sale -- 2,000 acres in New York state offered to colored farmers
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Advertisement signed by Samuel E. Cornish (dated New York, March 20 [1827]) offering 2,000 acres of land in New York state, within 70 miles of New York City on the banks of the Delaware River, for sale to colored settlers at less than half its ordinary value ($6/acre versus a going rate of $35), to encourage the formation of a colored farming settlement.
articles.notes: An early instance of Cornish personally promoting Black land ownership/settlement, predating similar 1840s efforts (e.g. the Smith lands in the Adirondacks).

articles.headline: Chilicothe, Ohio meeting in aid of a benevolent/educational society
articles.summary: Meeting held Monday evening, Feb. 19, 1827, at which Lewis Woodson was called to the chair and Wm. Dailey appointed secretary. After bye-laws and a constitution were read and adopted, resolved that Rev. William Graham deliver an address to aid the society, at the Presbyterian Church, with a collection to be lifted; citizens of Chilicothe, both white and coloured, invited to attend.
articles.notes: Lewis Woodson later became a prominent Pittsburgh AME Methodist minister, educator, and early Black nationalist writer ('Augustine'); this Chilicothe meeting predates his Pittsburgh career.

events.name: Boston meeting of the People of Colour in aid of the Freedom's Journal
events.location: house of Mr. David Walker, Boston
events.description: Meeting of Boston's People of Colour to consider giving aid and support to the newly founded Freedom's Journal; resolutions of support adopted and ordered published in the paper's first number.

events.name: African Association for Mutual Relief -- seventeenth anniversary celebration
events.description: Seventeenth anniversary celebration of the African Association for Mutual Relief at Zion Church; members of the Brooklyn Woolman, Union, Clarkson, and Wilberforce Societies attended in procession with their standards and badges. Prayers offered and addresses delivered.

events.name: Chilicothe, Ohio meeting in aid of a benevolent/educational society
events.description: Meeting of a benevolent/educational society (bye-laws and constitution adopted); resolved that Rev. William Graham deliver a fundraising address at the Presbyterian Church on the second Monday of April; both white and coloured citizens of Chilicothe invited.

events.name: African Mutual Relief Society -- extra meeting notice
events.location: African Mutual Relief Society Hall, No. 44 Orange-street, New York
events.description: Notice to members of the African Mutual Relief Society of an extra meeting called for Monday evening, April 16, 1827, by order of the President.
